data.title: Adidas: A Buy At Undemanding Valuations As Inventory Set To Normalize
data.description:
Adidas (ADDYY) (ADDDF) has been able to deliver a strong recovery to its top line over the past year, but the quality of that growth remains a point of debate, which has definitely weighed on ADR's lackluster performance over the same period.

The "brand of the three stripes" has shown a dynamic where inventory is increasing faster than sales growth, and its cash flow has been highly influenced by shifts in working capital. The main question is whether the turnaround has been driven by real demand or through balance sheet expansion.

While it is difficult to state precisely that Adidas trades in deep-value territory, I believe that at least it is possible to argue that the market has priced ADDYY as if the recovery will fall short. The market's skepticism of the management team's comments that inventory growth will normalize and that Adidas can sustain high-single-digit top-line growth for the next three years is unexciting. However, I believe that, especially trading in the current undemanding multiples, Adidas finds itself in a position that does not need to outperform to re-rate, only to execute.

Therefore, I'm leaning constructive on Adidas' thesis today, seeing some asymmetry as the trend of margin normalization progresses toward the company's guidance.

Inventory, Cash Flow, and the Quality of Growth

The first thing I look at in Adidas' thesis is whether the growth thesis is today being financed in a healthy way or "forced" via inventory and working capital.

When we look at the last ten quarters reported by Adidas, we can see strong revenue growth in the double digits, especially from September 2024 until now (with the exception of September 2025), but at the same time, extremely volatile operating profit in the same period. That alone tells a story that growth has not been "clean" or consistent, and the margin still seems to be far from stabilizing.

As a kind of "rule of thumb", ideally, inventory levels should not grow more than sales for a long time but at least in line with revenue, or rather, below it (which indicates efficiency). The chart below helps to illustrate well how Adidas' correlation between revenue and inventory growth has been since 2Q23.

In 2023, Adidas was going through a classic problem of overstocking (strongly negative inventory, ~-20%, and weak revenues), clearing collections (Yeezy), putting pressure on margins, and hindering healthy growth.

Normalization came in 2024, with inventories going to positive levels of growth, reaching sort of the "sweet spot" as the stock seemed to be rebuilding with demand (the best moment of the cycle, and no wonder recovery on ADDYY trading performance).

But in 2025, a yellow flag was raised again as inventory accelerated again more than revenue (~30% vs. ~10-15%), signaling an overbuilding scenario again, but which can be interpreted as (1) anticipating demand that may not come or (2) replenishing channels and betting on strong growth ahead.

In addition, the other chart below (which crosses cash from operations, changes in inventory, and net income) shows that Adidas' cash flow generation has been much more volatile than its net income, which also reinforces the relevant impact of working capital. It can be noted that throughout 2023 and parts of 2024, changes in working capital dominated the behavior of cash flows, with periods in which the release (or consumption of inventory) significantly distorted the operating flow without a direct correspondence with the performance of net income.

Perhaps the most evident point of this distortion was in 4Q24, when operating cash flow showed a sharp drop at the same time that profit did not collapse in the same way—i.e., indications that the problem was much more linked to working capital management than operations per se. FY25 was marked by a relevant recovery in operating cash flows, even though inventory variations continued to consume cash, but also suggested that other components of working capital are sustaining this recovery.

Interpreting the Inventory Rebuild

My initial reading of the correlation between revenue growth vs. inventory is that Adidas is potentially entering a risk zone again.

However, the management team highlighted in the last earnings call that this inventory growth is not a problem but a preparation for growth.

CFO Harm Ohlmeyer made it clear that Adidas "has been too disciplined on inventory in '23... we went too low" and is now preparing for further top-line growth in '26, based on three clear reasons listed: (1) World Cup build-up; (2) supply chain timing (earlier inbounds at year-end); (3) "clean" inventory with only 7% not being from the current season.

The most reassuring statement is along the lines that this disconnect between revenues and inventory will not be maintained for long:

“So rest assured that for the quarters to come, we will bring that [inventory levels] further down. And you should assume that we make progress in the first half of '26. And you can take me serious at year-end '26, you will see a number that is below the EUR 5.8 billion.”

For FY26, management is driving revenues by guiding high single digits and operating profits by ~€2.3 billion. It's not bad growth, but it's a slowdown from the 13% YoY sales growth reported in FY25.

Incorporated in USD, this growth is expected by consensus to be around ~5%, which is about 3% lower than what was guided before 4Q25. Worse than that is the more extended guidance until 2028 at the same levels of top-line growth and an EBIT margin expansion still being a "promise", showing a slower turnaround perspective.

At the same time, it is possible that Adidas is being too conservative in its guidance. But if demand fails to materialize—as current 2026 expectations suggest—the thesis of risk of inventory growth above sales growth returns to discount and margin pressure.

So I would say that, to a large extent, the underperformance of Adidas' ADS is in line with the fact that the market has not fully bought into the company's turnaround story.

Valuation, Risks, and What Comes Next

It seems very clear to me that Adidas is being priced as a still problematic business and far from something normalized. The company trades at a forward P/E of 13.3x, and a forward EV/EBITDA multiple of 7.8x—all well below its main peers, Nike (NKE) (~33x P/E; ~21.9x fwd EV/EBITDA), On Holdings (ONON) (~19.8x P/E; ~11.8x fwd EV/EBITDA), and Deckers (DECK) (~13.6 P/E; ~8.8x fwd EV/EBITDA).

But according to Adidas' own guidance, with EBIT expected to go from ~€2.1B to ~€2.3B and margins heading towards ~10+, the prognosis is for strong profit expansion in the coming years—perhaps slightly below the previously expected pace.

If we put a visibility for P/E FY2 and FY3 of 9x and 6.8x, respectively, these multiples are very low if Adidas were to deliver its guidance at all. That being said, if Adidas merely manages to stabilize margins, deliver top-line growth in high-single digits while at the same time normalizing inventory, I see a plausible path for Adidas to go to a multiple of ~18-22x (peer range).

So much so that if we take into account the consensus that long-term EPS tends to grow at a CAGR of 14.15% over the next three to five years, Adidas today trades at a PEG of 0.94, indicating undervaluation.

This suggests that Adidas is in a position where the bar is so low that even outperforming is not necessary for a re-rating, just a normalization scenario—and I think that's plausible.

Of course, execution is key, and there are also exogenous factors (macro and geopolitical) in addition to tariff impacts and FX challenges that may delay normalization towards the proposed guidance.

However, I believe that, as much as Adidas' thesis today is under great skepticism, the undemanding valuations for a business that seems to be reorganizing itself for a normalization over the next 1.5/2 years (in terms of margin growth) appear to offer an attractive entry point.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PVAL: Cautiously Optimistic Owing To Recent Outperformance, Factor Mix, Buy Rating Maintained
data.description:
In my view, the Putnam Focused Large Cap Value ETF (PVAL), an active, non-transparent exchange-traded fund I initiated coverage of in November 2025, still deserves a Buy rating, with a few caveats though.

PVAL has significantly outperformed the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) since my previous article, finishing ahead of this bellwether ETF in four calendar months in a row, as it was perfectly prepared for the capital rotation to cheaper, cyclical names that was the dominant market narrative before the U.S.-Israel-Iran conflict started (that is, February 28).

In my recent article on the Invesco S&P MidCap Value with Momentum ETF (XMVM) commenting on value stocks, I have written that

Some selectiveness in this space might reward investors as inexpensiveness could secure a more comfortable drawdown compared to the market.

And I believe PVAL does possess that type of "selectiveness" and nimbleness necessary to minimize the potential drawdown (if it happens) and not just keep pace with the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF during the possible recovery but also deliver some alpha into 2027.

In other words, while I still believe it is possible for the S&P 500 to deliver a high single-digit gain in 2026, I expect the bellwether index to continue drifting lower in the coming days (unless quick de-escalation is achieved, but I view this as unlikely for now) under the pressure from expensive oil and inflation fears. In this environment, as a value play with a 4.7% earnings yield, PVAL is solidly positioned as it enjoys a decent margin of safety. Also, depending on the trajectory of the conflict, I expect the S&P 500 to rebound in the second half of the year, and PVAL also has a considerable chance to exceed its pace, delivering a 10%+ gain in 2026.

With that being said, I believe there is still a risk that PVAL could face a significant price decline, and investors should be prepared for a 2022-like drawdown (that is, -15.3% as of September 2022) or worse in case the Iran war drives economic ripple effects well beyond oil price in triple digits (i.e., prolonged supply chain disruption, painful inflation surprises stemming from expensive distillates, including diesel, gasoline, and fuel oil, and other direct and indirect consequences of expensive hydrocarbons, etc.)

PVAL Strategy And Portfolio

To recap, as explained on the Franklin Templeton website, PVAL

...seeks capital growth and current income. The fund invests mainly in common stocks of U.S. companies, with a focus on value stocks that offer the potential for capital growth, current income, or both.

In the prospectus, it is explained that the fund favors large caps, which

... are of a size similar to those in the Russell 1000 Value Index.

As per the fact sheet, this index is PVAL's benchmark.

Keep In Mind That PVAL Is An ANT ETF

As an active, non-transparent ETF, PVAL has the luxury of not publishing the exact holdings dataset each business day. Instead, they are shared only monthly. So, investors who are accustomed to checking their ETFs' holdings daily considering whether investment decisions these funds made resonate with them or not, have only 'tracking basket,' which should have some overlap with the actual holdings, to assess. As explained by PVAL, this basket is

...composed of select recently disclosed portfolio holdings, liquid U.S. ETFs that convey information about the types of instruments in which the fund invests, and cash and cash equivalents.

As I have already explained in a few articles in the past, the ANT structure has both advantages and vulnerabilities. For example, as I wrote in November,

...traders who have strategies based on copying funds' most promising ideas (the result can be these funds' top ideas failing, which is not pleasant) have close to zero chances here (alas, not zero).

At the same time, as explained in the PVAL prospectus,

You may have to pay more money to trade the ETF’s shares. This ETF will provide less information to traders, who tend to charge more for trades when they have less information.

More details on the risks can be found in the prospectus.

PVAL Portfolio

A remark I ought to make here is that given PVAL is an ANT ETF, the most recent version of the portfolio we currently can dissect is as of February 28. In this regard, please keep in mind that the sector and factor exposures are theoretical, and the actual characteristics may or may not be different.

So, as of February 28, in the PVAL portfolio, there were 45 equities and one ETF, namely the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD). The latter accounted for 1.6% of the net assets. There was also a sizable cash position (2.53% of the net assets).

How has the portfolio changed since September 2025 (the version I discussed in November)? There were no tectonic shifts in composition, but something has changed in terms of style factors.

More specifically, my analysis shows that PVAL removed only one stock, American International Group, Inc. (AIG), and added one stock, The Allstate Corporation (ALL), and IWD. Companies that are present in the top five cohort could be found in the September portfolio as well, with comparatively similar weights. And compared to IWD, PVAL is overweight in all of them.

Company Ticker Weight in PVAL, as of February 28, 2026 Weight in PVAL, as of September 30, 2025 Weight in IWD, as of March 24, 2026 Sector Market Cap, $ Billion, as of March 25
Citigroup Inc. (C) 4.01% 4.18% 0.53% Financials 198.97
Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO) 4.01% 3.04% 1.06% Information Technology 319.39
Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) 3.98% 3.33% 2.30% Energy 689.10
Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) 3.40% 3.01% 1.97% Communication 3,506.71
McKesson Corporation (MCK) 3.36% 2.98% 0.03% Health Care 108.03

Created by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

In terms of sectors, PVAL has financials, health care, and industrials as the top three allocations. Compared to IWD (PVAL has about 82.9% overlap with it), it is more bullish on five sectors, including consumer discretionary. At the same time, it is notably underweight in communication and information technology, among others.

Please take notice that I have not included data for aggregate underlying holdings of IWD that is also present in the PVAL portfolio.

Now, let us discuss the factor story. I have added data for the IVV ETF, which I usually use as a market proxy, to ensure a broader context. The rationale here is that 90.4% of PVAL's holdings are S&P 500 constituents, as per my calculations.

What I like most about it is that PVAL is offering rather solid exposure to the value factor without compromising much on quality (one of the most important factors at this juncture) and growth.

  • For instance, its weighted average earnings yield (with no adjustments made as all companies in its portfolio are profitable) is 1.2x higher than that of IVV while the Price/Sales ratio is more than 2x lower.
  • At the same time, its WA forward growth rates have improved since November, especially the EPS growth rate. And even though they are meaningfully lower than IVV's, these rates are certainly healthy.
  • PVAL's allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Profitability grade or higher is quite significant, as it has even risen since November by 27 bps.

Besides, while I have never been particularly optimistic about the low volatility factor (as it has a strong tendency to underperform the market during recoveries), I believe the 0.85 weighted average 24-month beta of the PVAL portfolio is beneficial in the current environment as it should help it to minimize the downside capture.

PVAL Performance

As I have already said, PVAL's performance has been fairly robust, which is one of the primary contributors to my cautious optimism on the name.

For instance, since November 1, the ETF has delivered a 6.85% total return while the S&P 500 index has declined.

Importantly, since March 2 (the first trading day since the U.S.-Israel-Iran conflict started), PVAL has not only outperformed IVV but also the iShares S&P 500 Value ETF (IVE), the Invesco S&P 500 Pure Value ETF (RPV), the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV), and IWD, which hints (but, obviously, not guarantees) that in a scenario with the conflict lasting longer, it should do better not only than the market but also than its value-focused peers.

Looking at the longer periods, we see that since its inception in May 2021, PVAL has solidly outperformed the market I proxy with IVV as well as IVE, IWD, and RPV.

Next, over the June 2021–February 2026 period, PVAL not only had the highest CAGR in the group but also the strongest risk-adjusted returns (the Sharpe and Sortino ratios).

And what is especially relevant today is that its maximum drawdown (September 2022, -15.32%) was not that deep compared to not only IVV but also other value ETFs. Besides, it had the smallest downside capture ratio in the group of only 80.31%.

Risks

I am by no means advocating for the view that PVAL is completely riskless and it will deliver gains no matter what the market narrative is. Even though the historical data shows that it is capable of minimizing drawdowns and the downside capture, the current version of the portfolio has decent valuation characteristics and quality, plus, as an actively managed ETF, PVAL has the flexibility to recalibrate exposures quickly to adapt to the changing market conditions, the fact is, it is still vulnerable to a meaningful drawdown given the uniquely stressful macro conditions that are reminiscent of the pandemic and 2022.

I am watching the trajectory of the U.S.-Israel-Iran conflict closely, and I do believe that the longer the Strait of Hormuz question is not resolved, the more severe economic repercussions will be, potentially even leading to the interest rate hikes, not cuts. Higher rates, I do believe, will inevitably open the door for a bear market, decimating both stocks and bonds. In this scenario, PVAL might deliver a maximum drawdown similar to 2022 (i.e., in the mid-teens) or even deeper, as historical performance is important for hypothesizing, but it never guarantees anything. Plus, this time, there is a risk that low beta of its portfolio might constrain its upside capture, which might translate into underperformance versus IVV during recovery.

Final Thoughts

To conclude, medium- to longer-term, I remain moderately bullish on PVAL, and I expect it to outperform IVV (i.e., deliver a 10%+ gain in 2026). The ETF has been capable of delivering solid outperformance versus IVV since its inception and since my November 2025 article. I like how it manages risk (especially its downside capture ratio) and find its factor mix mostly appealing.

Nevertheless, it is necessary to be prepared for a significant drawdown since the uncertainty surrounding the Iran war is high and the consequences of the long war for equities can be severe.

data.title: SCHG: Excellent High-Quality Growth ETF Comes With Big Short-Term Risks
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG) is down 10% YTD, but despite decelerating EPS surprises and higher energy costs, Wall Street analysts aren't budging. They're still predicting 23.36% aggregate EPS growth for SCHG, which is similar to the 24.12% figure from January, and haven't reacted to the fact that EPS surprises for SCHG's top ten holdings were half the level they were the prior quarter. To me, this is a mistake that's not yet priced in, and an extra quarter of weak EPS surprises combined with higher inflation would likely lead me to issue a rare "sell" rating on SCHG in the coming months.

That said, long-term investors will likely view this as short-term noise. While I'm cautious about SCHG's current setup, I will highlight metrics that shows how its portfolio is of substantially higher quality than its value counterpart, and how high-quality portfolios have consistently done well over the last 20 years. Therefore, I still rate SCHG a "hold,", but the rating is subject to change, and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

SCHG Overview

Strategy Discussion and Key Fund Facts

SCHG has a 0.04% expense ratio, has $49 billion in assets, and tracks the Dow Jones U.S. Large-Cap Growth Total Stock Market Index, which selects large-cap stocks with the following six characteristics:

  • high projected P/E ratio
  • high historical P/B ratio
  • high projected three-to-five year operating EPS growth
  • high trailing five-year annualized sales growth
  • high 21-quarter annualized EPS growth
  • low indicated dividend yield

The Index is float-adjusted market-cap-weighted, with individual security weights limited to 24% (and an aggregate 48% for those with weights above 4.5%). Reconstitutions are annual in September, and you'll notice that the screens aren't only growth-oriented. In actuality, SCHG's Index purposely selects poorly-valued stocks that don't pay dividends. That's why it has only 1.01% overlap with the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), and, by design, no overlap with the Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Value ETF (SCHV).

While high valuations are typically a red flag, there's no denying that SCHG's strategy picks some of the highest quality stocks on the U.S. market. As shown below, Schwab's website lists a 34.87% portfolio-level return on equity as of February 28, 2026, compared to just 20.21% for SCHV. Therefore, from a factor perspective, investing in SCHG gets you strong exposure to the growth and quality factors, whereas SCHV promotes value and low volatility, based on the differences in the ETF's three-year standard deviation of returns (12.23% for SCHV, 15.22% for SCHG).

Why SCHG's High Quality Gives It A Big Advantage Over Value Investors

It's my longstanding belief that quality stocks outperform over the long run. There will be ups and downs, but if your time horizon is reasonably long, I believe quality-first investors will come out ahead. This gives SCHG an automatic advantage over SCHV, and to demonstrate the strength of the factor over the long run, please consider the following table I created using the information provided at the Ken French Data Library. These are the results of cap-weighted portfolios formed based on operating profitability, and they show that over the last 20 years, a portfolio of the top 30% of highest-quality stocks outperformed the middle 40% and bottom 30% portfolios by 2.56% and 4.61% per year, respectively.

These results are the reason why I double-weight the quality factor in my ETF Rankings System. I especially like that the highest-quality stocks still hold up reasonably well even under periods of significant market stress, like during the Great Financial Crisis in 2007-2008. Speculative low-quality stocks sometimes do better in recoveries, but they rarely outperform in consecutive years, so I don't feel they're appropriate for long-term investors. That's for the Cathie Wood-type investor, and that's not my style.

SCHG Performance Analysis

Are Growth Stocks Really Riskier? It Depends On Your Time Horizon

Since January 2010, SCHG has delivered an annualized total return (including dividends) of 15.43% compared to 11.11% for SCHV. In the table below, you'll see how SCHG also outperformed on risk-adjusted returns (Sharpe ratio) and downside risk-adjusted returns (Sortino Ratio). Furthermore, its long-term standard deviation of returns wasn't too much higher than SCHV's either.

The sole critique is its 31.80% max drawdown over the first three quarters of 2022, which was about 6% worse than SCHV's max drawdown during the Q1 2020 COVID-19 crash. This is evidence that large-cap growth stocks are riskier than large-cap value stocks, but what's interesting is that this risk deficiency disappears when you extend the holding period. As shown, the absolute worst two-year return that SCHG investors experienced was a 6.52% annualized loss, which isn't that much worse than SCHV's 5.53%.

If you don't tend to panic when markets decline, there's a decent argument that SCHG is no riskier than SCHV. To my earlier point, I believe that's more of a function of its superior quality rather than growth vs. value, and while things may have been different decades ago, there's no doubt that in the current environment, quality and growth go hand-in-hand.

How Has SCHG Performed Against Other Large-Cap Growth ETFs?

While SCHG may serve as a core ETF in your portfolio, it's simply one of many large-cap growth ETFs available to investors. I track 116, including 23 with at least a ten-year track record. Below is a summary of how SCHG has performed against those since April 2016, and as you can see, its 17.00% annualized return ranks #5/23 on the list. SCHG also ranks #5/23 on risk-adjusted returns (Sharpe and Sortino ratios).

It's not immediately apparent, but if you're familiar with a few of these tickers, an interesting pattern emerges. At a high level, the best performing ETFs are cap-weighted like SCHG, while the relatively poor returns for funds like QQQE and QQEW prove that equal-weighting stocks in the name of diversification isn't always a smart move. Again, I link this to quality. While it's questionable if Nvidia (NVDA) should comprise more than 10% of an ETF, the solution shouldn't be to knock its allocation down to 1% and redistribute it to lower margin stocks. You have to be careful that you're not creating a problem (e.g., lowering quality) when trying to solve another (e.g., improving diversification).

SCHG: Composition and Top Ten Holdings

The following table highlights the sector allocation differences between SCHG, the Invesco QQQ ETF (QQQ), and the iShares Russell Top 200 Growth ETF (IWY), which were the top performers over the last decade. One thing that stands out is that SCHG actually has the least exposure to tech stocks (44%) of the three, making up for it with about 4% and 2% more allocation to the Financials and Health Care sectors on average.

While SCHG appears better diversified at the sector level, I'd hardly call it a diversified ETF. After all, Nvidia (NVDA) and Apple (AAPL) comprise nearly 22% of the fund, and other Magnificent 7 stocks are round out its top ten, which comprise 57.59% of the portfolio.

Wall Street Is Too Complacent - A Key Risk For SCHG

In my investment thesis, I noted how SCHG's 23.36% next-year estimated EPS growth rate is virtually unchanged from its 24.12% rate in January. Given the uncertainty due to the Iran War and decelerating earnings surprises for many of the top stocks listed above (which I'll highlight next), I found this strange. However, it's actually been par for the course for at least the last two years. I went back through my archived files and found that SCHG's 1Y estimated EPS growth rate has been range-bound between 20-25% since July 2024, as has its 1Y historical EPS growth rate.

While there is variation among the individual stocks, it seems that Wall Street is content at just projecting that growth stocks, as a group, will continue on the same upward trajectory as before. To me, that's unrealistic, especially against the backdrop that SCHG's 23.39% 3Y EPS CAGR is unprecedented, and a healthy correction is probably warranted. I would also note that SCHG's next-year EPS growth rate when I reviewed it in December 2021 was 27.79%, and what followed was 0.44% in actual EPS growth and a 32% total return loss in 2022. In a nutshell, that's the risk SCHG investors face now: that EPS growth won't materialize, and that its P/E will continue to contract. Already since my review from January, SCHG's TTM P/E has dropped from 35.91x to 31.91x, or about 11%.

Another key risk is declining sentiment. Using data from Seeking Alpha, I've compiled the following table showing the EPS surprise trends over the last 16 quarters for SCHG's top ten holdings. On a weighted average basis, SCHG's top ten holdings delivered only a moderate 5.58% EPS surprise in Q4 2025 compared to 10.20% in the prior quarter. In addition, and for the first time in three years, only one stock (Tesla) delivered a double-digit EPS last quarter, and I view this as fundamental evidence that market enthusiasm is waning.

Is High Inflation Back?

Since the Iran War began on February 28, 2026, WTI Crude Oil Futures have jumped about $30 per barrel, and the price of jet fuel has roughly doubled, per IATA. As a result, the Center for Inflation Research now predicts a 0.55% month-over-month increase in PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred metric, for March 2026. Rate cuts this year, which would have a positive impact for growth stocks, are unlikely, given that the CME FedWatch Tool suggests they'll stay stable throughout 2026 at the current 350-375 level.

The last time oil prices were this high was in 2022, and turning back to the EPS surprise trends from earlier, you'll notice they were also low that year, even turning negative in Q3 and Q4. To reiterate, SCHG lost 31.80% that year, and I don't think it's a coincidence that when EPS surprises for SCHG's top holdings returned to double digits in 2023, the ETF rebounded with a 50.11% gain. Overall, these trends illustrate how sentiment can drive performance, so while SCHG remains an excellent long-term investment, I believe the downside risks right now are too high to justify new positions.

Investment Recommendation

SCHG is a popular large-cap growth ETF with a 0.04% expense ratio and $49 billion in assets under management. Large-cap growth stocks have a distinct quality advantage over their value counterparts, so taking your time horizon out of the equation for a moment, I expect SCHG will continue to outperform SCHV over the long run. That said, sentiment matters, and in SCHG's case, its top holdings' quarterly EPS surprises substantially decelerated in Q4 2025. In addition, the current high oil price environment makes a repeat of 2022 look possible, when EPS surprises were also weak and the ETF declined by 32%. Therefore, while SCHG still earns a long-term "hold" rating for its high quality features, I encourage investors to exercise caution at this point. Certainly, if EPS surprises decelerate further in Q1 2026, that would likely trigger a "sell" rating. Thank you for reading, and I look forward to your comments below.

data.title: Nike Earnings Preview: The Company Really Needs To Return To Mid-Single-Digit Revenue Growth
data.description:
Nike (NKE), the beleaguered sports footwear and apparel giant, reports their fiscal Q3 ’26 financial results after the closing bell on Tuesday, March 31st, 2026.

The stock has been the proverbial “house of pain” (hat tip to the Jim Cramer-ism), down -22% annualized from Nike’s $179 peak in late November ’21 to its current price of $52–$53 per share in late March ’26, versus the S&P 500’s +10% return for the same period.

The only conclusion that can be drawn was that Elliott Hill and his team were left with the proverbial “nuclear winter” landscape (certainly among footwear retail) when he assumed control from the former CEO, but the fact is Elliott has also made some progress at the retail level, getting Nike footwear (particularly Nike footwear) prominently reinstalled at DICK'S (DKS).

Current Street expectations for Nike’s fiscal Q3 ’26 are $11.23 billion in revenue, EPS of $0.28 and operating income of $512 million, for “expected” declines y-o-y of 0%, -48% and -31%, respectively.

For fiscal Q4 ’26, ended May ’26, Street consensus is looking for $11.3 billion in revenue to generate $386 million in operating income and $0.21 in EPS, for expected y-o-y growth of 2%, EPS growth of +50%, and operating income growth of +20%, but the grim prospect of the Q4 ’26 numbers is that the actual numbers remain sequentially down from fiscal Q3 ’26’s estimates, while fiscal Q4 is typically the strongest quarter of the year.

Jeffries recently put out a Nike update after DICK'S (DKS) quarterly results were reported, noting that DKS’s management was “unequivocally positive” on Nike’s strength in running innovation. (The Austin, Texas store was mentioned in one of the last two calls as perhaps being Nike’s epicenter of running innovation and improvement for the brand.)

My own anecdotal experience with DICK'S and Nike is that on Sunday, March 22nd, ’2026, I went into a DICK'S Sporting Goods store in Oak Brook/Lombard, Illinois, looking for some ice pack for the knees devices (old man), and the place was absolutely jammed.

I had trouble finding a parking spot, and the checkout lines were stretched well back into the store. Nike footwear and apparel were displayed prominently both on the main floor and on the upper, running-related floors.

EPS and revenue estimate revisions:

The EPS estimate revisions for Nike look back one year: note the drop after the tariff implementation in early ’25, from fiscal February, March ’26 and ’27 through the present.

The tariff comparisons get easier now, but there is still an impact. The bigger issue could be the relationship with China and how “unfriendly” China can make US companies as perceived by the Chinese citizens while selling US products into the Chinese market from a public relations perspective.

Nike revenue estimate revisions are unchanged over the last 15 months for fiscal ’26, ’27 and ’28. I would have preferred to see some mild upward drift to the estimates signaling at least low single-digit revenue growth.

Valuation

This has been written about in past Nike previews, but Nike’s peak valuation when the stock was trading at $179 in November ’21 was 50x EPS, 4.9x price-to-TTM revenue, and 40x and 43x cash flow and free cash flow.

Today, those same multiples are now 34x expected ’26 EPS for a -27% EPS y-o-y growth rate (or 23x for an expected 47% EPS growth rate in fiscal ’27), a 1.5x price-to-TTM-revenue valuation, and 30x and 28x cash flow and free cash flow (ex-cash).

Still a lofty valuation, but the price of the stock has come down, as have the numbers. The stock still isn’t cheap, with the exception of the dividend yield, which is now 3.15% (assuming a full-year dividend).

A technical look at the stock

Technically, Nike is very oversold on the Worden daily chart (bottom lower-right panel), and it is getting there on the weekly chart. I like the bottom with the early April ’25 lows near $52, but the stock cannot go lower than this after they report next Tuesday night, March 31.

Summary/conclusion

I’d like to put a valuation on Nike’s stock and say I think Nike is worth “X,” but I can’t without seeing some return to at least mid-single-digit revenue growth. Nike is like a bleeding wound that quarter after quarter seems to find another hole to dig itself into, and I’m talking about China and the present strained relationship with China, and the stock tends to slip lower after seemingly good news.

Nike is seeing some signs of progress with North America revenue (+9% y-o-y), led by the running franchise, but to give clients a historical perspective, China used to account for 20% of Nike's total revenue but a whopping 50% of its EBIT, but that EBIT percentage has now fallen to 19% from the peak around 50% in late 2021, 2022.

Again, here’s the consensus for “expected” y-o-y revenue growth, EPS and operating income for the all-important Nike fiscal Q4 ’26 to be reported late June ’26:

  • Revenue +2% y-o-y
  • EPS: +50% y-o-y
  • Operating income: +20% y-o-y

These y-o-y numbers are due to easier compares vs. the FY Q4 ’25, not to better growth in the business model, which is ultimately needed.

From a portfolio construction standpoint, this blog never owned Nike in the last 20 years until it dropped down to around $130 in early ’23, where it was initially bought, but the stock was then sold and then repurchased after it went through $100.

Nike has been a huge disappointment, but the stocks abrogation of the retail market by the past management team and allowing numerous sneaker brands, like Hoka, Skechers, and the slip-on tennis shoe evolution, to grab all that shelf and market share really was an unmitigated disaster for the brand.

Nike is still owned, given its non-correlated price movement with the post-Covid, 2020 bull market. Elliott Hill, in my opinion, is more Mark Parker than John Donahoe, and I suspect Elliott Hill and his team can return the Nike brand, at least partially, to part of its former glory.

Any sign that the stock will hold in the low-to-mid $50s on Tuesday night, March 31, ’26, will be a big technical positive.

None of this is advice, recommendation, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Readers should gauge their own comfort with portfolio volatility, and adjust accordingly. LSEG is the sole source of this blog’s EPS and revenue estimates for both the S&P 500 and individual stocks.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: PayPal: Keeping It As A Possible Convexity Play For Two Reasons (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
PayPal Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:PYPL) is a widely debated stock due to factors such as its inconsistent performance in recent years and a pending shift in its corporate strategy. I previously covered PayPal in March, 2025, where I communicated a cautious outlook to readers on the basis of discretionary spending headwinds and the stock's questionable pricing metrics. Despite my previously cautious outlook, I've decided to turn to a bullish outlook on PayPal's stock; here's why.

Reason 1: Oversold and Possibly Underestimated

PayPal has shed over 80% of its market value in the past 5-years. Its underperformance started in late 2021 amid a lack of conviction in its growth trajectory. In my view, investors grew discontent with the company's lack of new account growth. In addition, a bear market unfolded in 2022; it seems as though PayPal never recovered afterwards due to a lack of optimism among investors.

Figure 2 illustrates PayPal's historical stock performance. The stock rallied intensely during the COVID-era tech boom. However, I think investors eventually priced froth out of the market and ultimately decided to avoid PayPal's stock after the 2022 bear market ended. I believe investors neglected the stock post bear market due to PayPal's relatively lower new account monetisation. Moreover, a credit scare occurred within the banking industry in 2022/23, which likely contributed to PayPal's demise, seeing as it rolled out a Buy-Now-Pay-Later program in the years leading up to the credit stress environment.

Roll forward to 2026 and we see PayPal's stock trading at $44.21 per share, slightly above its listing price in 2015. I think most of PayPal's recent bad news has been priced-in and it's time to start looking at a possible reversal. However, the question beckons: Does PayPal have enough fundamental strength to recover from its lows and/or will a catalyst emerge? I think so; here's why.

PayPal has decided to enter a new regime by hiring Enrique Lores. However, let's focus on incumbent performance before discussing Lores. As illustrated by figure 3, PayPal's nominal growth isn't declining. The company's FX-neutral total payment volume increased by 6% during fiscal 2025 amid rising consistency in consumer demand as well as its omni-channel set-up and low-fee solutions (Venmo). The company has successfully translated its higher volumes into revenue growth, delivering 4% year-over-year growth during fiscal 2025, accommodated by transaction margin growth of 6% and non-GAAP EPS of $5.31 (+14% year-over-year). In my view, PayPal's omni-channel and synergies from integrated platforms have led to consolidated growth.

Let's take a closer look at PayPal's fundamentals.

The company currently derives around 43% of its revenue from international transactions, which I find highly appealing, given the emergence of capital fragmentation in the geo-political landscape (PayPal might provide a workaround) and the growing representation of off-shored contract work. I see tremendous opportunity in cross-border flow and international payments. However, I'm slightly concerned by PayPal's take-rate of 1.82%, which has been flat since it broke below 2% in March 2021. Take rates refer to the amount of transactions that PayPal retains as revenue from fees. A declining take rate shows that power might've shifted to merchants amid rising competition in the digital payments arena, which PayPal owns ~45% of (data may differ between vendors). Despite a declining trend in take rates, we've seen PayPal enhance footprint, volume, and total revenue growth on a consistent basis in recent years. In addition, I think the company's Buy Now Pay Later offering will eventually find its place - consumer credit can be highly cyclical and market consolidation is challenging – if mastered, we could see BNPL contribute massively.

Collectively, PayPal's fundamentals have translated into appetising profitability metrics, which can be used for both fundamental analysis as well as stock screening. For example, on a 5-year normalised basis, we see PayPal's return on common equity at 20.13% without any signs of deterioration. Furthermore, the company's 5-year average EBITDA margin of 19.45% is attractive and in-line with the sector median.

We've seen price multiple compression occur since PayPal's stock started selling off in late 2021. Similar to profitability ratios, multiples can be used for both fundamental analysis and stock screening. Many avoid using P/E multiples for tech companies due to the subjective nature of amortisation accounting and tech firms' inconsistent capital structures. However, I think PayPal can be priced on a P/E multiple due to its mature capital structure and its consistent depreciation & amortization schedule (which has ranged between 2.9% - 3.6% of net revenue in the past three fiscal years). Using that logic, we can see that PayPal's forward-GAAP P/E ratio of 8.78x is substantially lower than its 5-year average of 31.22x. In addition, PayPal's price-to-cash flow, price-to-sales, and EV/EBITDA ratios are all materially lower than their 5-year averages. Lastly, I point to the fact that PayPal's balance sheet illustrates a current ratio of 1.29x and $8.05 billion in cash & equivalents (which covers ~13.44% of its total liabilities), suggesting low liquidity risk, meaning the company can weather a storm if needed, re-invest internally for growth, or acquire externally for market share. In addition, solvency risk arguably remains intact, echoed by PayPal's total assets/total liabilities ratio of ~1.34 (a distance-to-default proxy); sustainable EBITDA and a commendable distance-to-default can manage both credit and equity investors' perception of the business' real risk.

If PayPal's Fundamentals Are Commendable. Then why is it selling off?

I would say that PayPal's recent underperformance can be attributed to a combination of recency bias from investors and complacency from the company itself. Regarding the latter, PayPal seems content with sitting on its market share instead of expanding aggresively, conveyed by the fact that its CapEx is relatively low versus its shareholder distribution amount (PayPal has guided toward $1 billion in CapEx for 2026 and $6 billion in shareholder returns). While shareholder returns can be accretive, it might ultimately be dilutive when a company distributes as opposed to re-investing during a period of intense competition – In my view, competition in the digital payments environment is rising; I think investors believe PayPal needs to re-invest to sustain its market share. Furthermore, I partially attribute PayPal's lacklustre stock performance to a potential recency bias from investors. PayPal's recent growth is steady, yet lower than the growth market participants had become accustomed to in the company's earlier days. For example, I point to figure 8, which shows that PayPal's 5-year revenue and EBITDA growth has levelled off considerably versus the ten year compound annual growth horizon. While I understand the discontent with slower growth, I think investors have over-emphasised the fact; an 80%+ 5-year sell-off is arguably exaggerated.

Reason 2: A Possible Acquisition Target

Rumours have emerged that PayPal might be an acquisition target. For example, recent reports linked Stripe with a takeover bid, driven by the fact that PayPal's market capitalisation has dropped to ~40.7 billion, which is arguably quite low for a company generating over $33 billion in sales, especially if we consider the company's telling market share, abundance of intellectual property (workforce and industry knowledge), and synergy prospects.

Side note: Figure 9 breaks down PayPal's enterprise value.

Why do I consider PayPal an acquisition target?

I don't think Stripe is the only company who would be interested in acquiring PayPal. In fact, I'd argue that mega cap companies such as Amazon (AMZN) who carry growing representation might be interested due to market share gain and synergies. Furthermore, speculation has emerged that Enrique Lores, PayPal's new CEO, might deploy a streamlining strategy during his tenure at PayPal. I think an execution-first strategy might add marginal value to the company, providing acquirers with a solid roll-up opportunity, whereby they can incorporate the infrastructure as-in, as opposed to worrying about loose-ends. In addition, streamlining can make PayPal a more alluring takeover target. Why? Well, a growth-by-acquisition strategy might've inflated the balance sheet. Instead, a stream-lined strategy turns PayPal into a more nimble organisation, allowing for a cleaner set-up in takeover bids down-the-line.

My acquisition target argument isn't central to my bullish thesis. However, I deem it a factor worth considering.

Unmentioned Risk Factors

I mentioned a few risks throughout the article. However, I wanted to highlight a few additional risks in a discreet section.

PayPal's Streamlining Might Backfire

As previously mentioned, PayPal's streamlining can result in sustainability of profitability metrics. In addition, streamlining can trim the balance sheet, leaving it as a more alluring acquisition target. That being said, streamlining always runs the risk of market share decay, especially during an era of rising competition in the digital payments arena.

What Goes Down Doesn't Always Come Back Up

Though often heterogeneously interpreted, hysteresis refers to a permanent effect on a variable resulting from an event. In my other words, it refers to a permanent change in equilibrium. As such, I highlight the fact that PayPal's stock won't revert to mean by default; the market might've simply priced a new equilibrium.

Final Word

I'm hanging on to my PayPal stock. Despite the myriad of headwinds faced by the company in recent years, a case can be made that investors have exaggerated the near-term slowdown in growth while ignoring features such as PayPal's market share, profitability, and normalised valuation multiples. In addition, a subtle case can be made for PayPal as an acquisition target, adding a secondary layer to my bullish outlook.

I label PayPal a convexity play because I believe its downside risk is mostly priced; a catalyst might lead to telling upside.

data.title: U.S. Inflation Is Rising, The Fed Is Waiting, And Forecasts Are Getting Weaker
data.description:
By Krzysztof Kamiński

Economists have clearly downgraded their outlook for the U.S. economy, concluding that the war with Iran and rising energy prices will weigh increasingly heavily on economic activity in the coming months. At the center of these concerns are higher inflation, weaker consumption, slower GDP growth, and a deteriorating labor market.

More and more often, the conflict in the Middle East is being viewed not as a temporary disruption, but as a new inflationary shock that could make it harder to maintain a balance between growth and price stability.

Inflation is once again becoming a bigger problem

According to a Bloomberg survey, the average forecast for growth in the PCE index - one of the most important measures of inflation in the U.S. - has risen for this year to 3.1% from the previous 2.6%.

This is a significant shift, showing that economists are beginning to assume a more lasting impact of higher energy prices on prices across the entire economy. An additional warning signal comes from the OECD, which expects average inflation in G20 countries to reach 4% this year, whereas as recently as December it had projected 2.8%.

At present, the conflict is having its strongest impact through the fuel and energy markets. Gasoline prices in the U.S. have risen by more than 30% this month, to around $4 per gallon, marking the biggest jump since Hurricane Katrina in 2005.

Higher fuel and transportation costs are already increasing the burden on households, and in the coming months they may also translate into higher food prices and prices for other consumer goods. There are also concerns about fertilizer shortages, which could push prices in stores even higher.

Economic growth is weakening, and the labor market is sending worse signals

The worsening outlook is also visible in economic growth forecasts. Economists now expect U.S. GDP to grow by 2.3% this year, compared with the earlier forecast of 2.5%. The scale of the revision does not yet suggest a sharp downturn, but it does confirm that the economy is beginning to feel the effects of rising energy costs and greater uncertainty more clearly.

Weaker forecasts also apply to the labor market. The expected average monthly number of new jobs has been revised down to 43,000 from 70,000, while the average unemployment rate is projected to reach 4.5%. This means that the labor market should remain relatively stable, but the pace of improvement is expected to be much weaker than previously assumed.

At the same time, the probability of a recession over the next 12 months has increased from 25% to 30%, showing that concerns about the economy’s condition are becoming more widespread.

The Fed may wait longer before cutting interest rates

Higher inflation and greater uncertainty have also affected expectations for the Federal Reserve. Economists have pushed back their forecast for the first interest rate cut to September, concluding that the Fed will need more time to assess whether the rise in energy prices is temporary or whether it is beginning to spread more permanently throughout the economy.

This is an important change, because until recently the market had been counting on faster monetary easing that could support consumption and investment.

Wall Street is lowering expectations, but it is not yet talking about a crisis

A more cautious view is also evident among the largest financial institutions. Goldman Sachs assesses the risk of recession at 30% and assumes that the unemployment rate will rise to 4.6% by the end of 2026.

Morgan Stanley, meanwhile, has lowered its forecast for consumption growth in 2026 to 1.7% from 2.0%, indicating that higher energy prices will largely offset the positive impact of larger tax refunds.

Even so, a deep downturn scenario is not yet dominant. Some economists believe that the U.S. economy may still grow at around 2% in 2026, mainly thanks to investments in data centers and the artificial intelligence sector.

These are areas less dependent on expensive imported energy than traditional industries. However, this scenario is resting on an increasingly narrow foundation, as it depends mainly on sustained investor optimism toward AI and on the resilience of spending by wealthier consumers.

Consumption is still holding up, but that resilience may be limited

So far, consumer behavior data do not show a clear breakdown. JPMorgan and Bank of America indicate that by mid-March there were no strong signs of a slowdown in credit card spending. This suggests that American households are still maintaining relatively solid purchasing activity.

Economists note, however, that even a quick end to the conflict would not necessarily mean a quick disappearance of its effects. The impact on the oil market, transportation, and production costs may persist much longer, sustaining inflationary pressure and limiting the strength of any rebound.

The U.S. economy is becoming more fragile

The current picture of the American economy does not yet point to a sharp deterioration in conditions, but it clearly shows the system’s growing fragility. Higher energy prices are pushing inflation upward, weaker labor market and GDP forecasts are weighing on sentiment, and the Fed may be forced to maintain restrictive monetary policy for longer.

As a result, the U.S. economy remains on a growth path, but it is increasingly dependent on a few narrow pillars, such as AI investment and the relative resilience of consumption. The war with Iran is therefore beginning to act as a new destabilizing force - one that is not yet triggering a recession, but is clearly increasing the risk that the economy will become less resilient to future shocks.

data.title: Consumer Sentiment Plunges 6% Amid Energy Price Spikes And Market Volatility
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Driven by a volatile mix of escalating gas prices and financial market instability, consumer sentiment plunged nearly 6% in March to its lowest level since late 2025.

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index fell 3.7 points to 53.3, missing the 55.5 forecast and snapping a four-month streak of gains as consumers grapple with the fallout from the ongoing conflict in Iran. This sharp decline places current sentiment in the bottom 1st percentile of the series' history.

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index is a monthly survey of consumer confidence levels in the U.S. with regard to the economy, personal finances, business conditions, and buying conditions, conducted by the University of Michigan. There are two reports released each month: a preliminary report released mid-month and a final report released at the end of the month.

Joanne Hsu, the director of surveys, made the following comments:

Consumer sentiment fell back 6% this month to its lowest level since December 2025. Declines were seen across age and political party. Consumers with middle and higher incomes and stock wealth, buffeted by both escalating gas prices and volatile financial markets in the wake of the Iran conflict, exhibited particularly large drops in sentiment. Overall, the short-run economic outlook plunged 14%, and year-ahead expected personal finances sank 10%, while declines in long-run expectations were more subdued. These patterns suggest that, at this time, consumers may not expect recent negative developments to persist far into the future. These views are subject to change, however, if the Iran conflict becomes protracted or if higher energy prices pass through to overall inflation. Interviews for this release were collected between February 17 and March 23, with about two-thirds completed after the start of the US military conflict in Iran.

A Historical Perspective on Recession Risk

The chart below provides a long-term perspective on this widely watched indicator. We have highlighted the index's value at the start of each recession. The current level of 53.3 is below the index's value at the start of all six recessions since its inception.

To put today’s report in historical context, consumer sentiment is currently 36.5% below its average reading of 84.0 (arithmetic mean) and 35.6% below its geometric mean of 82.8, based on data dating back to 1978.

To visualize the volatility and its impact on the broader economy, the following chart includes a three-month moving average and real GDP. Historically, prolonged periods where the moving average remains at these depressed levels have closely correlated with negative GDP growth (the red bars below).

The Political and Presidential Lens

Each month, the survey results highlight sentiment within each political party. Sentiment is often viewed through a partisan lens, but the data shows that sentiment has fluctuated both positively and negatively under both Republican and Democratic administrations.

As the chart below illustrates, the current "plunge" is a rare moment of bipartisan agreement, with declines seen across the political spectrum as energy costs hit every household.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Components

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index consists of two sub-indexes: the Current Economic Conditions Index (CECI) and the Consumer Expectations Index (CEI). The CECI reflects consumers' views of their current financial situation and the overall economy, while the CEI gauges their outlook for the future.

While both measures fell in March, a concerning gap is opening between how consumers feel today and what they fear for tomorrow.

  • Current Economic Conditions Index (CECI) fell for the first time in three months to 55.8, a 1.4% decline from the previous month and a 12.5% drop from a year ago. The latest reading was lower than the forecast of 57.8.
  • Consumer Expectations (CEI) fell a second straight month to 51.7, an 8.7% decline from the previous month and a 1.7% drop from one year ago. The latest reading was lower than the forecast of 54.1.

The steeper decline in the Expectations Index suggests that while consumers are managing current costs, they are bracing for a much harsher economic environment in the months ahead.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Inflation Expectations

Year-ahead inflation expectations climbed from 3.4% in February to 3.8% this month, the largest one-month increase since April 2025. The current reading exceeds those seen in 2024 and remains well above the 2.3-3.0% range seen in the two years pre-pandemic. Long-run inflation expectations inched down to 3.2%. In 2024, values ranged between 2.8% and 3.2%, while in 2019 and 2020, they were consistently below 2.8%. Note that for both time horizons, interviews completed after February 28th exhibited higher inflation expectations than those completed before that date.

Other Sentiment Indicators

For an additional perspective on consumer attitudes, see the most recent Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index. Both indexes gauge consumer attitudes toward the current and future strength of the economy. However, the Consumer Confidence Index is more influenced by employment and labor market conditions, while the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on household finances and the impact of inflation.

The Conference Board index is the more volatile of the two, but the broad pattern and general trends have been remarkably similar to the Michigan index.

And finally, the prevailing mood of the Michigan survey is also similar to the mood of small business owners, as captured by the NFIB Business Optimism Index (monthly update here).

ETFs associated with sentiment include Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY).

Original Post
