data.title: How Too Many Drivers Spoil UGL
data.description:
Investment Thesis

ProShares Ultra Gold ETF (UGL) is a classic example of how too many good cooks can spoil the broth. Gold’s monster movement in the last 12-18 months was caused by not one or two but four entangled factors. These four drivers are rates falling or expected rate cuts, massive central bank price-agnostic buying, numerous geopolitical risks, wars, etc., and dollar debasement. Even a single one of these factors is enough to move gold up. When you combine them, you get a motley group of forces - some of them tangential if not downright opposed to each other. That dynamic is enough to cause loss of momentum sometimes.

For gold itself, that occasional loss of momentum is not a problem because gold moves primarily on direction, not path. For a 2x daily leveraged ETF like UGL, though, even a little loss of gold’s momentum in a bullish market creates a situation where gold moves in a zigzag manner - even if broadly upwards. That sort of choppiness works fine for physical gold but is fatal for daily leveraged ETFs because they follow the path, not the direction of movement. This is why, after a terrific year where it saw a ~77% rise, UGL is down ~30% this month.

My thesis is that, due to both structural reasons and the effect of macro factors on that structure, UGL will not bounce back easily. The structural constraints of UGL are well-known. The macro reason, which I will discuss here, is the presence of too many drivers at once. Unless there’s a clean source of momentum, momentum will not be steady, and since I believe that is not going to happen soon, I rate UGL a sell.

Why These Drivers No Longer Work Together

Take rates: rates are not falling in a straight line anymore. Inflation is not moving in any direction evenly, and policy—or at least its expectation—keeps shifting. One authority talks about rate cuts, another equally strong authority discusses pauses.

The same uncertainty exists with the dollar. It is no longer cleanly weakening. At times, it strengthens when there are risk-off flows and relative growth. At other times, it weakens on policy expectations. So what we get is a push-pull dynamic, not a consistent tailwind.

So is the geopolitical situation. It is not all constantly bad. Yes, risk is definitely elevated, but they are episodic, not cumulative. Sometimes one event escalates, and markets react sharply, like in the ongoing Iran conflict today. Another major event fades from public perception, or from headlines—the Russia-Ukraine war, Venezuela, and President Maduro—and that softens the momentum for gold. So what we get are short bursts of momentum, not sustained trends.

Perhaps the most stable of these four drivers is central bank buying. However, it is also the least sensitive to price. That means it provides a floor, one below which the gold price will not fall. But it fails to provide a directional push.

Put together, these drivers no longer reinforce each other. They take turns dominating the narrative, often in opposite directions. The result is not a breakdown in the gold thesis, but a breakdown in its consistency. Gold is no longer moving in a clean trend but in a sequence of reactions.

That distinction is critical. A single dominant driver creates persistence. Multiple competing drivers create volatility.

Why That Is Specifically A Problem For UGL

UGL is designed for persistence, not for alternating momentum.

When gold is driven by a single dominant factor, its upward movement is sustained and consistent. Here, UGL’s 2x leveraged daily reset structure does its job as intended, amplifying the returns. However, when so many competing forces line up and gold’s upward movement becomes erratic, UGL’s structure amplifies not that direction, but that erratic path.

This is how UGL starts underperforming. Since its returns are tied to daily movements, not overall trends, alternating gains and losses in gold only create losses in UGL. Over time, this gets compounded.

UGL Is Built For Direction - Not Volatility

UGL’s problem is not gold’s broad direction, which is still moving upward, but its own daily-return structure. The fund targets twice the daily return of the Bloomberg Gold Subindex. That means gold’s erratic upward path has a telling effect on UGL’s returns.

UGL is not facing some minor tracking issue—the issue is structural and consistent with the very nature of leveraged daily return ETFs. Such returns are path dependent, so even if the broad direction of gold is positive, daily movement patterns—gold going up and down intraday - will hurt UGL’s returns.

This is straightforward: suppose gold moves +5% one day and -5% the next day; it ends slightly down, say ~-0.25%. This is basic math. A 2x leveraged ETF just compounds this loss, so it becomes -0.5% or thereabouts. Keep on doing this every few days, and the gap between UGL and physical gold or plain gold ETFs keeps widening.

So when we have two things, higher volatility and longer holding periods, returns tend to be worse than the benchmark. That is exactly the setup we are now in.

On top of that structural drag, how UGL tracks gold is also responsible for its performance. It does not hold physical gold but uses futures contracts as well as swap agreements. Now, futures contracts expire and have to be repurchased, often at higher prices. This is called roll cost.

Roll cost shows up as a measurable drag over time. In a typical contango environment, each roll can cause a small loss because each future contract becomes more expensive than the earlier one.

Moving on, swap agreements are done with third-party institutions; they are not directly held. This introduces counterparty costs and reliability concerns. Tracking friction is the difference between holding gold and holding a derivative like a futures contract; this gets compounded over time. Add the 0.95% expense ratio, and what you have is not a direct macro view of gold, but something more besides that. That extra cost degrades returns significantly over time, unless you are buying at the right time and under the best volatility conditions. Additionally, holding this ETF for more than a day has its own risks that include volatility drag, on which more can be found here.

Why Bounce-Backs Will Be Short-Lived

A basic counter would be: if gold is still broadly bullish, why wouldn’t UGL simply recover along with it?

To that, I would say gold rebounds are likely going to be fragmented, not sustained. Each driver of its momentum—rates, geopolitics, central bank buying, and currency weakness—operates on its own timeline. One may turn supportive, but since they are all entangled with each other in complex ways, they will not all become simultaneously supportive.

So now we get a start-stop pattern. We see gold rally sharply on rate cut talk, then stall as yields stabilize. It can spike when bombs are falling, and it can retrace when another ship crosses the Strait of Hormuz safely, leaving gold modestly upward moving but dragging UGL down due to its daily structure.

The current environment is very different, with its volatility and sentiment swings. Leveraged products perform poorly in such times.

Why A Single Dominant Driver Is Unlikely

UGL needs a cleaner, less diversified macro trigger. This cycle saw too many powerful triggers, some bearish, some bullish. The result was choppy movement. Gold may still be fine, but choppiness is fatal for UGL.

These are not just multiple drivers of gold. They are conflicting and competing drivers.

Take rates and inflation as an example. Gold goes typically bullish when real yields decline. But real yields will now decline when central banks ease policy, and they will do that if inflation stabilizes. If inflation does not stabilize, rate cuts are not going to happen now. On the other hand, if growth weakens, there will be rate cuts, but there will be parallel risk-off moves that will make the dollar stronger. So rate cuts will drive gold higher, but a strong dollar will drag it down. So even within this one driver, the path is not linear.

Now let’s look at the dollar, which often trades in the opposite direction to gold. However, the dollar itself is being driven by too many things - rate expectations, differences in growth, and geopolitical risk sentiment. This is creating a situation where gold and the dollar are actually moving together. That adds to the inconsistency.

The key problem is not a multiplicity of drivers, but that they are fundamentally different in time and by their functions. Monetary policy is slow-moving and inflation-driven. Geopolitical events are increasingly whimsical, moving in sudden bursts, then fading. Currency markets react to both broad geopolitics and policy expectations. So, they react to two different things that have no correlation to each other. Central banks buy hundreds of tons of gold, no matter the price. So they offer support but do not push.

Because these are so different, I don’t believe their natural tendency is to align. Even when they do align, it is incidental and does not persist. That structural mismatch makes sustained alignment unlikely, not just absent at the moment.

What Would Change My View

Three things need to happen together for UGL to work properly—path, positioning, and cleaner triggers. One, gold must move up, and do so in an orderly fashion. Leveraged products with daily-reset characteristics need not just the right direction, but the right path. When gold rises up in a straight line, UGL can do its job well, giving you nearly 2x daily return.

The single dominant driver that could do this and control the narrative could be real yields. This is my most plausible candidate. A sustained and decisive decline in real yields could take hold of the gold movement and make it trending. Not intermittent rate cuts, but a persistent, planned easing cycle combined with stable inflation. This could create a consistent tailwind for gold.

The other requirement is positioning. Gold got its recent support from a powerful narrative—falling rates, central bank buying, geopolitics, and dollar debasement. However, the narrative became widely understood, so there was the crowd, and then a little loss of momentum got amplified.

This needs to change. Gold must stop being so interesting again for UGL to work properly. That reset has to come from either a larger correction, or we just have to give it time. Until that reset happens, though, UGL remains vulnerable to crowded, profit-taking, flow-driven swings.

Conclusion

There is no doubt that UGL’s returns have been strong. But the setup has now shifted. Gold is now over-owned, a widely held macro trade, and we can see that in the price action. UGL is not built for this kind of environment.

It doesn’t matter if gold drifts higher - many investors will say that, but that is not the true UGL investment argument. What matters is whether gold is taking a zigzag path upwards. Since that is happening, UGL is not the right gold instrument.

UGL will probably become attractive again when gold trends consistently upward. This is not that time. So this is not UGL’s time.

Until there is a single dominant driver pushing gold in a sustained way, and until positioning resets enough to allow that trend to build cleanly, UGL remains structurally disadvantaged.

Since I don’t believe this set of four drivers will align in the near future, I consider UGL a clear SELL.

data.title: Credit Markets Are Still Risk-On: Why We're Calling A Strong Sell
data.description:
Thesis

This Seeking Alpha article is our tool to warn investors of the risks we see in the corporate bond market, especially in situations where we see a high probability for those risks to materialise. We argue that investors should consider high expected inflation numbers, current low spreads compared to the last +20 years, and the low credit quality of investment-grade bond ETFs when allocating their portfolio. We initiate with a STRONG SELL on corporate bonds as an asset class by analyzing in detail multiple popular ETFs with that exposure.

Credit Spreads

In our previous article, The Silent Risk In Portfolios: Long-Duration Exposure, we argued that the unfavorable maturity distribution of US Treasury debt, high US budget deficits, and inflation expectations derived from breakeven rates are in favor of selling long-duration Treasury and buying ultra-short duration Treasury. Since the bond market is generally priced with a spread on risk-free, i.e., Treasury bonds in the case of US corporate bonds, in this article, we will look at the corporate bond ETFs and then give our final verdict on the total bond market ETFs.

The chart below shows corporate bond spreads over time vs. Treasury 10-year bonds on a yield basis. As the data clearly shows, all stated spreads per rating category are far below its median levels for the period. Spread on US corporate A-rated securities is 1.02% vs the median of 0.78% or 24 bps below the historic median. Spread on BBB/Baa corporates is at 1.12% vs the median of 1.61% or 49 bps below the median, and spread on HY is 2.99% vs 3.85% median or 86 bps below the median.

So, the lower the credit quality, the greater the deviation from the median. In our view, this shows that the corporate bond market is still in a risk-on environment, no matter the poor returns over the last couple of years that have happened due to the bear market in Treasuries. Also, please note that in the case of market stress, spreads can move quickly over the median line, and during the recession (marked on the chart in red), they can reach more than 1.000 bps.

In our view, this data shows that investors are not adequately compensated for the risk in the corporate bond market, although the current yield, which may be much higher than in previous years, may feel much better.

So, let us check the most popular corporate bond ETF by investment-grade categories.

Investment Grade Corporate Bonds ETFs

Investment-grade corporate bonds are bonds issued by companies with credit ratings of BBB- or higher by S&P Global Ratings by S&P Global Inc. (SPGI) and by privately held Fitch Ratings, or Baa3 or higher by Moody's provided by Moody's Corporation (MCO), indicating relatively low default risk. They typically offer lower yields than high-yield bonds, reflecting stronger balance sheets with more stable cash flows and a higher probability of timely debt repayment.

The most popular ETFs in this category are Vanguard Short-Term Corporate Bond Idx Fd ETF (VCSH), iShares iBoxx $ Inv Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD) and iShares Broad USD Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (USIG) and the smaller one PIMCO Investment Grade Corporate Bond Index ETF (CORP).

iShares iBoxx $ Inv Grade Corporate Bond ETF

This is ETF with total net assets of $30 billion, with more details in the following table.

Please be aware that the allocation of the fund is at the lower end of the investment grade credit rating, with a BBB - rated corporates making more than 40% of the total allocation. By our calculation stated before, BBB - rated corporate bonds would need to rise in yields for about 49 bps just to get in line with the median. In a case of severe market stress, spreads would widen even further, and in the case of recession, we would expect a large number of downgrades and defaults that would be a serious drag on the investors.

Due to our view of bear-steepening US Treasuries yield curve, historically low spreads, and low quality of the investment grade bonds, we rate iShares iBoxx $ Inv Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD) as STRONG SELL.

iShares Broad USD Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF

This is the fund with about $17 billion of total net assets. Compared to the previous one, it offers much larger diversification with 11.4 k holdings vs 3.1 k on the previous one. It has a slightly shorter duration of 6.37 years compared to 7.92. In our view, the duration difference is not large enough to influence investor decisions in a meaningful way when comparing the two. The average YTM of 5.13% vs 5.27% on the latter is also not that significant.

What worries us the most is that this ETF is even more tilted to the verge of investment grade, with a BBB-rated bonds containing 45% of the total exposure. Top, AAA-rated bonds are less than 1% of the exposure.

Same as in the previous case, due to our view of bear-steepening US Treasuries yield curve, historically low spreads, and low quality of the investment-grade bonds in the in the assets, we initiate iShares Broad USD Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (USIG) with STRONG SELL.

PIMCO Investment Grade Corporate Bond Index ETF

We stated in the previous part of the article that this is a much smaller ETF compared to the previous two, with a net asset value of $1.6 billion. The duration of 6.5 years is not much different from the previous ones, same as the YTM of 5.27%.

However, here we find a more granular view of the portfolio's credit rating.

Although stated as a corporate bond ETF, the ETF holds 8% of government-related securities. We believe this is due to more flexibility for PM-s since it is a smaller ETF, and since most of the bonds are traded in minimal lots (100 thousand or more), and as a source of quick liquidity. Like its stated peers, however, it has a poor credit quality with 43.85 % of total assets in the BBB-rated range. What is most interesting is that 10.68% of the ETF assets are at the very edge of investment grade, at BBB-. Also, a disproportionately large part of the not-rated securities of more that 15% is a source of concern per se, in our view.

Due to the same reasons as previous ETFs, we initiate PIMCO Investment Grade Corporate Bond Index ETF (CORP) as STRONG SELL.

Vanguard Short-Term Corporate Bond Idx Fd ETF

This ETF has total net assets of $48.3 billion according to the latest data available. In bond strategies, short-term is often considered 1-3 years, so this fund, with an average maturity of 2.9 years and an average duration of 2.7, is on the upper limit of short-term. We believe this is to enhance yield, which currently states 4.2%.

Same as the previous examples, the fund is largely exposed to the BBB-rated segment of the bond market with 46.05% of total exposure. AAA-rated securities are 0.31% of the total fund, something we find to be too aggressive for this kind of ETF.

Due to the same reasons as previous ETFs, we initiate Vanguard Short-Term Corporate Bond Idx Fd ETF (VCSH) with STRONG SELL.

High-Yield Corporate Bond ETFs

High-yield corporate bonds are debt securities issued by companies with below investment-grade credit ratings, offering higher yields to compensate for elevated credit risk. The limit for high-yield corporate bonds is a credit rating below investment grade, which is defined as lower than BBB− by Standard & Poor's and Fitch Ratings, or lower than Baa3 by Moody's Corporation (MCO). They are typically issued by firms with higher leverage or less stable cash flows, which increases the probability of default.

The most popular ETF in this category is iShares Broad USD High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (USHY).

iShares Broad USD High Yield Corporate Bond ETF

This ETF has total net assets of $24.5 billion. The risk of the portfolio is mitigated partially by its lower duration than in the case of investment-grade funds. Here, the duration is only 3 years, while the average YTM is 7.32%. However, one needs to be careful while taking YTM into account because, in the case of any bond default, the real YTM will be lower.

Unlike the investment-grade ETFs, where fund assets are exposed to the lower part of the stated range, here we can see that the majority of exposure is to the upper-tier of HY space, with BB-rated bonds making more than half of the portfolio.

However, since we believe that HY issuers are at high risk of raising interest rates as well as widening spreads, which could make the cost of debt hard to service, we view iShares Broad USD High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (USHY) as STRONG SELL. We simply do not think that in a situation where inflation is expected to rise substantially and where HY spreads are under the LT median, investors are not compensated enough for the risk they would take on with this product.

Broad Bond Market ETFs

Finally, broad bond market ETFs are the ones that combine security and liquidity from Treasuries that make up about 3/4 of total assets and higher yield from corporates that make up the rest of the portfolio. The most popular ETF in this category is the iShares Core US Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG).

iShares Core US Aggregate Bond ETF

This is ETF with total assets of $138 billion, duration of 5.76 years and average YTM of 4.6%. Since the bear market in US Treasuries has lasted for some time, the fund has a momentum grade of D.

Although we can see the selling point of these kinds of products, with the majority of assets in Treasuries with enhanced risk from corporates that still puts it in the bond and not equity bucket, we believe that the current situation we mentioned before (rising inflation, US unfavorable debt maturities, and corporate spreads under the median) exposes bond investors to equity-like risk they need to be aware of. As shown in the rating quality allocation, BBB-rated bonds account for 11.87% of the portfolio.

Due to everything mentioned above, we initiate iShares Core US Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG) with STRONG SELL. Again, we urge investors to recognize equity-like risk from the BBB-rated part of the portfolio that can widen spreads and start to turn bad rather quickly in case of a recession.

Risk to our Bearish View

The main risk to our bearish view is a stark change of policy regarding budget spending and deficit. If policymakers decide to cut the deficit and cut spending in a prolonged and sustainable way, that would give the market more confidence that long-term inflation is manageable, and finally, it would give the Fed more room to cut rates. That would not only cut rates and lower the yields but possibly even cut spreads for corporate bonds.

The other risk to our view is the Fed controlling the curve and effectively cutting the rates while viewing the inflation rates as transitory. A major domestic economic shock would force the Fed to move preemptively and cut rates, no matter the current inflation levels. However, this is a risk to bear our bearish view, contingent on the move in Treasury yields and spreads not widening any further.

To wrap It Up

After we argued that the unfavorable maturity distribution of US Treasury debt, high US budget deficits, and inflation expectations derived from breakeven rates are in favor of selling long-duration Treasury and buying ultra-short-duration Treasury, we wanted to see what risks investors face when investing in corporate bonds. As this article clearly concludes, there are multiple layers of risk that investors may not be aware of. One of the risks is tight spreads across the different rating categories compared to the historical median. The lower the investment quality, the lower the spread compared to the median. The other important risk is that, as we clearly demonstrated, investment-grade corporate bond ETFs are largely exposed to the lowest part of the investment-grade universe that carries the highest yield but also the highest risk.

Also, we believe that HY corporate bond ETFs are disproportionately exposed to equity-like risks with widening spreads and rising inflation that can make large numbers of companies insolvent and make them default, so stated YTM on those ETFs may be much higher than yields investors will be able to realize with those ETFs.

Finally, due to everything stated in the article, we argue that investors in broad-based bond ETFs should realize the level of equity-like risks they are taking since the ETF carries a large part of the lowest investment-grade securities.

We initialize all stated ETFs, Vanguard Short-Term Corporate Bond Idx Fd ETF (VCSH), iShares iBoxx $ Inv Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD) and iShares Broad USD Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (USIG) and the smaller one PIMCO Investment Grade Corporate Bond Index ETF (CORP), iShares Broad USD High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (USHY) and iShares Core US Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG) as STRONG SELL.

data.title: Weekly Market Compass: No. 13, Geopolitical Risk Sets The Pace
data.description:
The past week has seen stock markets experience strong swings due to geopolitical events in the Middle East. The key event of the week could be President Trump's announcement that he will suspend attacks on Iranian oil facilities for five days and presented a 15-point peace plan to help resolve the conflict. Initially, there was an initial sense of optimism, which quickly turned to pessimism when Iranian leaders reacted negatively to the proposals (first denying negotiations and then rejecting them). Subsequently, Iranian leaders stated that they did not like the U.S. plans, but offered their own conditions for ending hostilities (5 points).

In this market phase, it seems that Iran wants to maintain its position as a strong geopolitical player, given that it has the ability to flip the switch in the Strait of Hormuz at will. These failed negotiations combined with news of a possible U.S. troop deployment caused investors to continue defending against rising equities.

The Big Picture

The S&P 500 (SP500) continues to decline from all-time highs, with the current correction initially triggered by labor market concerns and now exacerbated by the blockage of the Strait of Hormuz. The technical structure remains fragile and vulnerable to further shocks.

Geopolitical conflict has produced extraordinary price swings in commodities. Prices for crude oil (CL1:COM) fell dramatically based on speculation about the likelihood of a resolution to the conflict. As soon as it became apparent that no agreement had been made regarding a cease fire, the price of crude oil rose significantly. In addition to being uncertain, the price action in commodities demonstrates extreme volatility. Risk-averse investors have turned to traditional safe-haven investments as well as lower-yielding long-dated debt securities that could potentially be affected by higher oil prices and subsequent inflation.

It's also worth noting that precious metals are behaving differently than we would normally expect. They are no longer being used as typical Safe Haven Assets. There has been a tremendous amount of "Speculative Foam" built into the markets. Many investors bought these precious metals at the top of this recent price run-up using leverage (i.e., margin). Now, because there is so much uncertainty in the marketplace, many investors are having to rapidly unwind their leveraged positions. As a result, both gold (XAU) and silver (XAG) are currently the most easily liquidated asset classes for many managers trying to raise capital and immediately increase their portfolio liquidity reserves.

Crude oil prices continue to be positively positioned. Long dated treasury yields have increased, and Bitcoin has fallen further, which supports the view that investor sentiment is extremely risk adverse at present. Investors are experiencing a classic "defensive" shift in equity investment portfolios in response to this unique combination of geopolitical factors. Capital has exited over-valued sectors, including technology and communication services, and moved to other areas of the market where investors can find both income and stability that are typically not negatively impacted by geopolitical shocks or rising commodity prices.

Defensive and cycle-dependent sectors such as materials and utilities are dominating this week, in contrast communication services and technology have experienced heavy losses due to high valuations. It is also worth noting that weakness is systemic and spreading into the broader market. Market breadth is worsening as just 47.2% of the S&P 500 stocks are currently above their 200-day moving average. Generally, when less than half of the market participates in an upward movement, it indicates solid and broad weakness of the market structure.

Therefore, a general repricing of stock values occurring once again due to the geopolitical risk premium will force investors to reduce exposure.

Chart of the Week: Panic Over Oil Supplies (Extreme Backwardation)

This week, I am going to do a deeper dive into a key factor to help us understand why the panic in the energy markets is so severe; it’s known as the term structure of WTI crude oil futures.

From the picture above, we have two major lines. Blue is last month’s price curve and shows a relatively flat price structure. White is this week’s current price action and shows how white starts off very high for very short-dated maturities, peaking at approximately $100 per barrel, but then falls-off rapidly through each of the subsequent year's maturities.

Technical speaking, when short-dated prices (spot) are substantially higher than longer-dated prices (futures), the futures market is said to be in backwardation. On the other hand, if futures prices are slightly higher than spot prices to account for cost of storing and insuring the product, then the futures market is said to be in contango.

I view the extreme and steep backwardation seen in the current oil futures market, similar to past red flags for the world economy. What this extreme backwardation indicates to me is that oil consumers are willing to pay a tremendous premium to obtain barrels today. They fear that there will soon be a disruption in physical supplies caused by U.S.-Iranian tensions and threats against the Strait of Hormuz.

Until such time as the curve returns to its normal shape, the risk of another inflationary shock will cause concern with the Fed and lead to upward pressure on interest rates and limit any sustainable recovery in equity values.

The TurboQuant Enigma

The tech space is always an interesting place to watch markets react; last week was no different with Alphabet/Google (GOOGL) announcing a new AI called TurboQuant which is designed to accomplish all the functions previously done by its predecessors using six time less computer memory than before. And almost immediately after the announcement came a selloff of shares of several key memory component manufactures such as Micron (MU) and Samsung (SSNLF), as investors were fearful that since the new AI uses six time less memory that there will be significantly lower demand for physical chips.

If AI is going to become lighter and a lot cheaper to operate then it will likely be utilized far more than it would otherwise. AI can run on standard PCs and common hardware, and no longer do you have to tie your device to a server or spend an arm and leg. With this explosion of utilization of AI, we are bound to see an upward trend in total demand for those technological parts.

So in summary, the increased use of AI will create a huge surge in demand for these types of technological components, not less demand. Looking back at the Google stock chart I see that the drop in the share price has been extremely rapid and abnormal.

Using some technical terms, we could say that the RSI indicator has fallen into what’s known as oversold territory. To my way of thinking, the panic selling has gone too far and created a possible buying opportunity for those investors willing to take a longer view and ignore their emotions.

The Road Ahead

For the upcoming week, my outlook will be very guarded. The S&P 500 is currently below it’s 200 day moving average; as you know, historical trends suggest that when stocks trade below their 200 day moving averages, there could be a higher level of volatility than normal. Therefore, the primary focus of the week will be on the effects that the ongoing conflict with Iran has on inflation and what direction the Federal Reserve will take. There is also much scheduled activity on the economic calendar. Consumer Confidence data will be reported on Tuesday. Due to increasing gasoline prices and consequently increasing limitations on discretionary household spending, I do not anticipate an increase in consumer confidence.

Additionally, JOLTS (Job Openings And Labor Turnover Survey) job postings data for February is expected to reveal some carry-over strength in the labor market prior to the war. An estimate of 7 million jobs available is anticipated.

The ISM manufacturing index will be released on Wednesday. I am anticipating expansion in the sector to have occurred again in March, however, I will be watching the price component of the data closely. Energy cost increases globally are occurring as a result of the current energy shock.

The most significant event of the week will occur on Friday: the release of the employment report for March. Based on current trends and estimates, I expect non-farm payroll additions of approximately 80,000, resulting in a stable unemployment rate of 4.4 percent. Although markets appear to be pricing-in potential interest-rate hikes by the Federal Reserve during 2026, I believe those estimates may be overly optimistic based on future data indicating declines in demand in subsequent months.

In terms of currency and bonds, I expect the U.S. dollar to continue to gain traction as a safe-haven asset until evidence of a resolution occurs regarding the ongoing war. Concurrently, I anticipate that the flattening of the Treasury yield curve will continue into April if oil prices remain high throughout May, as investors increasingly view near-term inflation expectations as increasing.
